title: Gear trains, belts & power screws
summary: A focused toolkit for the motion-transmission exam problem: ordinary and epicyclic gear trains (Willis formula), belt drives via the Eytelwein/capstan law, ropes and raising devices, and power screws including efficiency, self-locking and the differential screw. Quick theory plus nine fully worked past-exam problems (49-57) with every number verified against the answer key.
objectives: Compute speed ratios and torques through ordinary multi-stage gear trains, including idle gears and the role of the constraint couple on a fixed casing.
Apply the Willis formula to epicyclic (planetary) trains: build the fixed-carrier ratio tau with correct external-mesh signs and solve for any shaft speed.
Use coaxiality conditions to find missing teeth numbers and explain why planetary reducers achieve very large ratios.
Solve flat- and V-belt drives with the Eytelwein/capstan equation, including the V-belt wedge factor and pretension/take-up tensioning.
Analyse power screws: helix angle, raising/lowering torque, efficiency, and the self-locking (irreversibility) criterion, including the differential screw.
Reduce inertias to a single shaft to find start-up accelerations and close bearing/support reactions with equilibrium checks.

blocks.text:
Almost every machine moves a load that turns slowly and forcefully from a motor that turns fast and weakly (or vice-versa). The job of a **transmission** is to change speed, torque and sometimes direction with high efficiency. Four families cover the whole exam:

- **Gear trains** (spur/helical gears in series): exact, compact ratios.
- **Epicyclic (planetary) trains**: huge ratios in a coaxial package, solved with the Willis formula.
- **Belts & ropes**: flexible drive limited by friction grip (Eytelwein) — they can slip.
- **Power screws**: turn rotation into a strong linear push; can be made self-locking.

The golden rule for all of them: with efficiency $\eta$, **power in = power out / efficiency** and **torque scales inversely with speed**.

$i=\dfrac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}}=\dfrac{n_{in}}{n_{out}}$. For a meshing gear pair $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\dfrac{z_2}{z_1}=\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}$ (teeth ratio = inverse radius ratio). For a multi-stage train, multiply the stage ratios. $i>1$ is a reducer (slows down, multiplies torque).

blocks.caption: Spur-gear basics: pitch radius from module m and teeth z; tangential force from torque; normal force along the line of action at pressure angle alpha; radial component.

blocks.text: **Ordinary trains.** Stack meshing pairs on intermediate shafts. The overall ratio is the product of stage ratios $i=\prod (z_{driven}/z_{driver})$. An **idle gear** (one between two others) changes only the sense of rotation, not the magnitude of the ratio. Torque on each shaft = (torque on previous shaft)$\times$(its stage ratio); the slow output shaft carries the largest torque.

In a planetary train the planet axes move (carried by the **planet carrier/arm** P or H). You cannot just multiply ratios. The trick: work in the reference frame of the carrier, where the train looks ordinary. Subtract the carrier speed from every gear.

blocks.caption: Willis: tau is the fixed-carrier ('basic') ratio between the two sun/annulus gears. e = number of EXTERNAL meshes (each external mesh gives a sign flip; internal/annulus meshes do not). Plug in the two known speeds, solve for the third.

blocks.text: Willis recipe: (1) identify the two end gears (1 and 4) and the carrier P. (2) Compute $\tau$ with the carrier imagined fixed, counting external meshes for the sign. (3) Write $\dfrac{n_1-n_P}{n_4-n_P}=\tau$ and substitute the two given speeds. (4) Solve linearly for the unknown. Coaxiality fixes the missing teeth count, e.g. for two suns sharing a carrier $z_1+z_2=z_3+z_4$.
Belts, ropes and the capstan equation

blocks.caption: Eytelwein / capstan law. T1 = tight side, T2 = slack side, f = friction coeff, theta = wrap angle (rad). A V-belt with half-groove angle gamma multiplies grip by 1/sin(gamma) (the wedge effect).

Transmitted power = tension difference times belt speed; torque on a pulley = tension difference times its radius. A pretension/take-up commonly fixes T1+T2 (or the slack T2); Eytelwein then gives the other tension at the slip limit.

blocks.text: Classic belt mistakes: (1) using degrees instead of radians for theta; (2) forgetting the V-belt factor 1/sin(gamma) — a 12 deg groove multiplies the effective friction by ~5; (3) applying the wrap angle of the WRONG pulley — slip starts on the pulley with the SMALLER f*theta, usually the small one.

blocks.caption: Helix angle lambda from lead p and mean diameter d_m; friction angle phi=arctan(f). Driving torque to raise a load Q. The lowering torque uses (lambda - phi).

blocks.text: A screw is **self-locking / irreversible** (the load cannot back-drive it) when $\lambda<\varphi_a$, i.e. the helix angle is below the stick friction angle $\varphi_a=\arctan f_a$. Raising **efficiency**: $\eta=\dfrac{\tan\lambda}{\tan(\lambda+\varphi)}$. A self-locking screw always has $\eta<0.5$.

blocks.title: Reliable solving recipe for a transmission problem

steps.text: Identify the kinematic chain and the ratio. Gears: multiply stage teeth ratios. Epicyclic: use Willis with the carrier. Belt: speeds equal at the contact, $i=D_2/D_1$. Screw: $v=\omega\,p/2\pi$.

Do statics/torque flow with efficiency. With eta=1, power and so $C_{out}=C_{in}\cdot i$. A fixed member (annulus, carter) carries a constraint couple $C_V$ found from torque balance $C_{in}+C_{out}+C_V=0$.
Get tooth/belt/thread forces: $F_t=C/r$, $F_n=F_t/\cos\alpha$ for gears; Eytelwein for belts; $Q\,(d_m/2)\tan(\lambda\pm\varphi)$ for screws.
Bearing reactions from the FBD of each shaft: sum forces and take moments about a support. Vertical equilibrium checks: reactions must support all weights plus rope/belt loads.
For dynamics (start-up), reduce all inertias to one shaft: $I_{eq}=I_{in}+\sum I_k/i_k^2+\sum m_k r_k^2/i_k^2$, then $C_{in}-C_{load,red}=I_{eq}\,\dot\omega$.

blocks.text: Memorize three workhorses: Willis $\dfrac{n_1-n_P}{n_4-n_P}=\tau$; Eytelwein $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}=e^{f\theta}$ (V-belt: $f/\sin\gamma$); screw $C=Q\dfrac{d_m}{2}\tan(\lambda+\varphi)$ with self-locking when $\lambda<\varphi_a$.

question.prompt: In an epicyclic train, how many sign flips does the fixed-carrier ratio tau pick up?

options.content: One per external mesh; internal (annulus) meshes give no flip
One per gear in the train
One per internal mesh; external meshes give no flip

question.explanation: Each external (gear-on-gear) mesh reverses rotation, so tau gets a factor of -1; an internal mesh (planet inside an annulus) keeps the same sense, so no flip. Count the external meshes to set the sign of tau.

question.prompt: A V-belt has half-groove angle gamma=12 deg and friction f=0.4. Its effective gripping friction coefficient is about:
question.explanation: The wedge multiplies grip by 1/sin(gamma): f' = 0.4/sin(12 deg) = 0.4/0.208 = 1.92. This is why V-belts transmit far more torque than flat belts of the same pretension.
